title: US PCE Hits 4.1% Three-Year High, Cementing Fed Rate Hike Bets for 2026
content: The Bureau of Economic Analysis reported on 25 June that headline PCE rose to 4.1% year-on-year in May — its highest since April 2023 — whilst core PCE accelerated to 3.4% YoY, the loftiest reading since October 2023, on a monthly gain of 0.4%. The data, combined with Chair Kevin Warsh's hawkish tone at the 17 June FOMC, has firmed expectations for at least one rate hike in 2026; futures markets now price near-certainty of action by December. Two-year UST yields climbed to 4.21%, their highest in over a year, and the 30-year briefly touched 5.2% — a level last seen in 2007 — before settling around 5.06%. Equity futures edged positive on release, though tech names remained under pressure.

title: Brent Slumps to $72 on Hormuz Recovery; Drone Strike Exposes Ceasefire Fragility
content: Brent crude settled at $71.99/bbl on 26 June — its lowest since late February and more than 10% below the prior week's close — whilst WTI fell below $70/bbl, as Persian Gulf oil flows continued to recover following the US-Iran interim peace deal signed on 17 June. Exports have recovered to roughly 75% of pre-conflict levels, with Saudi Arabia resuming tanker loadings at Ras Tanura and UAE, Kuwait, and Qatar lifting output. OPEC faces further fragmentation as Iraq threatened to exit over production quotas, following the UAE's departure in May. However, a drone strike on a cargo vessel in the Strait of Hormuz on 26 June rattled shipping markets, pushing war-risk premiums back towards 0.7% of hull value and raising questions about the ceasefire's durability.

title: AI Chip Rout Sends Kospi Down 10% in a Day; Asian Tech Markets Partially Rebound
content: South Korea's Kospi suffered its steepest single-session fall in recent memory on 24 June, plunging 10% as SK Hynix and Samsung Electronics both tumbled more than 12% amid concerns over AI hardware demand sustainability at elevated prices. The sell-off spread across the region: Hong Kong's Hang Seng fell 1.82%, mainland China's CSI 300 lost 2.77%, and Japan's Nikkei 225 shed 3.55%. US equity futures also weakened before paring losses. AI-exposed European semiconductor names came under pressure during the session. Markets staged a partial recovery on 25 June — Samsung gained over 9% and SK Hynix more than 4% — but the episode underscored the concentration risk embedded in AI-driven equity rallies and renewed questions about the durability of the semiconductor upcycle underpinning broader index gains.
